verdict_reasoning: HSPD1 (HSP60) is canonically a mitochondrial matrix chaperonin essential for protein folding in mitochondria. However, extensive literature documents its translocation to the cell surface under stress, oncogenic transformation, and cell-death conditions. On tumor cells, HSP60 is well-documented as a cancer ecto-chaperone displayed on the outer leaflet, making it a recognized target for antibody and immunotherapy approaches. During apoptosis and ICD, HSP60 translocates to the plasma membrane outer face. Heat/ER stress also induces surface display. The NCBI summary itself notes a signaling role in innate immunity consistent with surface exposure. No documented constitutive PM pool in baseline normal cells, so 'yes' is not warranted. Contextual cell-state-induced (cancer, stress, cell death) ecto-display is well-supported.
